title: Toolchain, CMake & Installing raylib
html:
What you are building toward

This part takes you from an empty folder to a distributable C++ game. The first job is the least glamorous and the most important: a working toolchain. A toolchain is a compiler (turns C++ into machine code), a build system (CMake — decides what to compile and how to link), and a library (raylib — the code that draws pixels, plays sound, and reads input for you). Get this right once and every later lesson just works.

Why raylib (and where SFML/SDL2 fit)

raylib is a small C library with a flat, friendly API: InitWindow, DrawRectangle, IsKeyDown. No inheritance, no engine, no scene graph — just functions. That makes it perfect for learning what a game actually does. We use it as our primary library.

  • SFML — a C++ library (classes like sf::RenderWindow, sf::Sprite). More object-oriented; great if you prefer C++ idioms. Same concepts apply.
  • SDL2 — lower level and ubiquitous (raylib itself can sit on top of it). You manage more yourself; choose it when you need maximum portability or are targeting consoles/mobile.

Everything you learn here transfers: a window, a loop, draw calls, input, delta time, and collision are universal.

Install a compiler

  • macOS: run xcode-select --install to get Clang. Install CMake with brew install cmake.
  • Windows: install MSYS2 then pacman -S mingw-w64-ucrt-x86_64-gcc mingw-w64-ucrt-x86_64-cmake, or use Visual Studio with the "Desktop development with C++" workload.
  • Linux: sudo apt install build-essential cmake git.

Verify everything is on your PATH before continuing:

terminal
$ cc --version Apple clang version 16.0.0 $ cmake --version cmake version 3.29.2 $ git --version git version 2.45.0

Let CMake fetch raylib for you

You could install raylib system-wide, but the most reproducible approach is FetchContent: CMake downloads and builds raylib as part of your project. No version surprises, works on every machine. Create this folder layout:

project layout
hello-raylib/ ├── CMakeLists.txt └── src/ └── main.cpp
CMakeLists.txt
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.16) project(hello_raylib CXX) set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD 17) set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD_REQUIRED ON) include(FetchContent) FetchContent_Declare( raylib GIT_REPOSITORY https://github.com/raysan5/raylib.git GIT_TAG 5.5 # pin a version, never "master" ) set(BUILD_EXAMPLES OFF CACHE BOOL "" FORCE) FetchContent_MakeAvailable(raylib) add_executable(game src/main.cpp) target_link_libraries(game PRIVATE raylib)
src/main.cpp
#include <cstdio> #include "raylib.h" int main() { // Prove the toolchain links raylib without opening a window yet. printf("raylib version: %s\n", RAYLIB_VERSION); printf("Toolchain OK \u2014 ready to make games!\n"); return 0; }

Configure and build

CMake works in two phases: configure (reads CMakeLists.txt, downloads raylib) then build (compiles and links). Run from the project root:

terminal
$ cmake -S . -B build -- Fetching raylib 5.5 ... done $ cmake --build build [100%] Built target game $ ./build/game raylib version: 5.5 Toolchain OK — ready to make games!
The first build is slow.
CMake compiles all of raylib once (a minute or two). Every build after is fast because it is cached in build/. Never commit build/ to git — add it to .gitignore.
"raylib.h not found".
You almost always forgot target_link_libraries(game PRIVATE raylib), or you ran cmake --build before re-running the configure step after editing CMakeLists.txt. On Linux you may also need system deps for the windowing backend: sudo apt install libgl1-mesa-dev libx11-dev libxrandr-dev libxi-dev libxinerama-dev libxcursor-dev.
Pin your dependencies.
Always set an explicit GIT_TAG (a release like 5.5), never a moving branch. Six months from now your build should still produce the same binary.
🎮 Build This: Hello Window Check

Prove your compiler + CMake + raylib all work together before writing any game code.

  1. Create the folder layout above with the three files.
  2. Run cmake -S . -B build and watch raylib download.
  3. Run cmake --build build until you see Built target game.
  4. Run the executable and confirm it prints the raylib version and the OK message.
  5. Add a .gitignore containing build/ and git init the folder.

title: Opening a Window & the Game Loop
html:
The shape of every game

Every real-time game is the same loop, forever, until the player quits:

  1. Input — what did the player press?
  2. Update — advance the world (move things, check rules).
  3. Render — draw the current state of the world to the screen.

raylib gives you three primitives to build that loop: InitWindow opens the window and creates the GPU context, WindowShouldClose() returns true when the user clicks the close button or you choose to exit, and BeginDrawing() / EndDrawing() wrap the render phase. EndDrawing is also where raylib swaps the back buffer to the screen and sleeps to hit your target frame rate.

The minimal complete program

main.cpp
#include "raylib.h" int main() { const int screenW = 800; const int screenH = 450; InitWindow(screenW, screenH, "Blank Canvas"); SetTargetFPS(60); // cap the loop at 60 frames per second while (!WindowShouldClose()) { // also true when ESC is pressed // --- update would go here --- BeginDrawing(); ClearBackground(RAYWHITE); // wipe last frame DrawText("Press ESC to quit", 250, 200, 20, DARKGRAY); EndDrawing(); } CloseWindow(); // free the GPU context and window return 0; }

Build and run it exactly as in Lesson 1. You get a resizable-looking 800x450 window titled "Blank Canvas" that fills with off-white every frame. By default raylib maps the ESC key to "should close", which is why WindowShouldClose() returns true when you tap it. That is the "closes cleanly" behavior for free.

Why ClearBackground every frame?

The window is a buffer of pixels that keeps whatever you drew last. If you never clear it, moving objects leave smeared trails. ClearBackground resets every pixel to a solid color first, giving you a clean slate. Always call it as the first thing inside BeginDrawing.

Colors and the coordinate system

raylib ships named Color constants: RAYWHITE, BLACK, RED, DARKBLUE, LIME, and more. Make your own with Color c = { 30, 30, 46, 255 }; (R, G, B, A, each 0–255). The screen origin (0,0) is the top-left corner; X grows right, Y grows down. This trips up people used to math graphs — a higher Y is lower on screen.

SetTargetFPS is not your timestep.
It caps how often the loop runs so you do not melt the CPU, but it does not guarantee 60. A slow machine drops frames. That is exactly why movement must use delta time (Lesson 4) instead of assuming a fixed frame rate.

Useful loop helpers

  • GetFPS() — current measured frames per second (great for a debug readout).
  • DrawFPS(x, y) — draws the FPS counter for you.
  • GetScreenWidth() / GetScreenHeight() — current window size.
  • SetConfigFlags(FLAG_VSYNC_HINT) — call before InitWindow to sync to the monitor refresh.
Order matters.
Call InitWindow before any draw or texture/sound function — those need the GPU/audio context that InitWindow creates. Anything between BeginDrawing and EndDrawing is a draw call; do not put your update logic there or you mix concerns and make bugs harder to find. And never forget EndDrawing() — without it the screen never updates and the app appears frozen.
One loop, three phases.
Keep input, update, and render visually separated in main even before you split them into functions. Future-you will thank present-you when the game grows.
🎮 Build This: Blank Canvas

A titled window that clears to a color each frame and quits cleanly on ESC.

  1. Start from the program above; rename the window title to your game's name.
  2. Change ClearBackground to a custom dark color like { 24, 24, 37, 255 }.
  3. Add DrawFPS(10, 10); inside the draw block and confirm it reads ~60.
  4. Run it, resize-test it, and press ESC to confirm the window closes and the program returns 0.

title: Drawing Shapes/Text & Reading Input
html:
Drawing primitives

raylib has a draw function for every basic shape. They all take screen coordinates and a Color:

draw calls
DrawRectangle(x, y, width, height, color); DrawRectangleRec(rectangle, color); // takes a Rectangle struct DrawCircle(centerX, centerY, radius, color); DrawCircleV(position, radius, color); // takes a Vector2 DrawLine(x1, y1, x2, y2, color); DrawText("text", x, y, fontSize, color); int w = MeasureText("text", fontSize); // pixel width, for centering

To center text horizontally, measure it and subtract half from the screen midpoint: DrawText(s, screenW/2 - MeasureText(s, 40)/2, y, 40, WHITE);.

Reading input

There are two flavors of input check, and the difference is everything:

  • IsKeyDown(KEY_W) — true every frame the key is held. Use for continuous motion (moving a paddle).
  • IsKeyPressed(KEY_SPACE) — true only on the frame the key goes down. Use for one-shot actions (jump, fire, pause).

Mouse mirrors this: IsMouseButtonDown(MOUSE_BUTTON_LEFT), IsMouseButtonPressed(...), and GetMousePosition() returns a Vector2. Key names are KEY_W, KEY_UP, KEY_SPACE, KEY_ESCAPE, etc.

Building Pong

Pong is two paddles and a ball that bounces inside the window. We track each object's position as plain variables and move the paddles with held keys. We will use raylib's Rectangle struct (x, y, width, height) for the paddles.

pong.cpp
#include "raylib.h" int main() { const int W = 800, H = 450; InitWindow(W, H, "Pong"); SetTargetFPS(60); Rectangle left = { 30, H/2 - 40, 15, 80 }; Rectangle right = { W-45, H/2 - 40, 15, 80 }; float paddleSpeed = 6.0f; Vector2 ball = { W/2.0f, H/2.0f }; Vector2 ballVel = { 5.0f, 4.0f }; float ballR = 9.0f; while (!WindowShouldClose()) { // --- input + update --- if (IsKeyDown(KEY_W) && left.y > 0) left.y -= paddleSpeed; if (IsKeyDown(KEY_S) && left.y < H-left.height) left.y += paddleSpeed; if (IsKeyDown(KEY_UP) && right.y > 0) right.y -= paddleSpeed; if (IsKeyDown(KEY_DOWN) && right.y < H-right.height) right.y += paddleSpeed; ball.x += ballVel.x; ball.y += ballVel.y; if (ball.y < ballR || ball.y > H - ballR) ballVel.y *= -1; // bounce top/bottom if (ball.x < 0 || ball.x > W) { // off the side: reset to center ball = { W/2.0f, H/2.0f }; } // --- render --- BeginDrawing(); ClearBackground(BLACK); DrawRectangleRec(left, RAYWHITE); DrawRectangleRec(right, RAYWHITE); DrawCircleV(ball, ballR, RAYWHITE); DrawLine(W/2, 0, W/2, H, GRAY); // center net EndDrawing(); } CloseWindow(); return 0; }

Run it: W/S drive the left paddle, the arrow keys drive the right, and the ball bounces off the top and bottom walls. Notice we clamp each paddle so it cannot leave the screen by checking left.y > 0 and left.y < H - left.height before moving.

Speed in pixels-per-frame is temporary.
We hardcode paddleSpeed = 6 and add it directly. That assumes 60 FPS. In Lesson 4 we switch to speed * GetFrameTime() so the game plays identically on any monitor. For now, fixed FPS keeps Pong simple.
Bounce can stick.
Reversing velocity when the ball is already past the wall can re-trigger next frame and "vibrate" the ball at the edge. The safe pattern is to also clamp the position back inside: if (ball.y < ballR) { ball.y = ballR; ballVel.y *= -1; }. We will lean on this in collision (Lesson 5).
🎮 Build This: Pong

Two paddles and a ball that bounces inside the window.

  1. Type in the program above and confirm both paddles move with their keys.
  2. Make the ball bounce off a paddle: if its rectangle contains the ball, flip ballVel.x (collision details come next lesson, but a quick X-overlap check works now).
  3. Add two integer score variables; increment one when the ball exits the opposite side, and draw both with DrawText + TextFormat("%d", score).
  4. Speed the ball up slightly each paddle hit for difficulty.

title: Vectors & Movement with Delta Time
html:
Vector2: position and direction in one type

raylib's Vector2 is just { float x; float y; }, but treating it as a single mathematical object makes movement code clean. A vector can mean a position (where something is) or a velocity (how fast and in what direction it moves). The raymath.h header gives you the operations:

raymath helpers
#include "raymath.h" Vector2 a = { 3, 4 }; Vector2 b = { 1, 0 }; Vector2 sum = Vector2Add(a, b); // { 4, 4 } Vector2 scaled= Vector2Scale(a, 2.0f); // { 6, 8 } float len = Vector2Length(a); // 5.0 (sqrt(3^2+4^2)) Vector2 dir = Vector2Normalize(a); // length 1, same direction float dist = Vector2Distance(a, b);

Normalizing is the key trick: a normalized vector has length 1, so it is pure direction. Multiply it by a speed to get a velocity that is the same magnitude in any direction — essential so diagonal movement is not faster than straight movement.

Delta time: the cure for frame-rate dependence

In Pong we wrote ball.x += 5. On a 60Hz screen that is 300 pixels/second; on a 144Hz screen it is 720 — the game runs more than twice as fast. The fix is to express speed in pixels per second and multiply by the time the last frame took:

delta time
float dt = GetFrameTime(); // seconds since last frame, e.g. 0.0166 at 60fps float speed = 300.0f; // pixels PER SECOND now ball.x += speed * dt; // always 300px/s regardless of FPS

At 60 FPS, dt ≈ 0.0167 so you move ~5px/frame; at 144 FPS, dt ≈ 0.0069 so you move ~2px/frame — but 144 of those add up to the same 300px in a second. Velocity-based movement becomes one clean line: pos = Vector2Add(pos, Vector2Scale(vel, dt));.

Building Breakout: paddle + moving ball + brick grid

We lay out a grid of bricks, a mouse- or key-controlled paddle, and a velocity-vector ball. Collision response is next lesson — here we get everything moving with delta time.

breakout.cpp (movement)
#include "raylib.h" #include "raymath.h" struct Brick { Rectangle rect; bool alive; }; int main() { const int W = 800, H = 600; InitWindow(W, H, "Breakout"); SetTargetFPS(144); Rectangle paddle = { W/2.0f - 50, H - 40, 100, 16 }; float paddleSpeed = 600.0f; // px/sec Vector2 ball = { W/2.0f, H/2.0f }; Vector2 ballVel = Vector2Scale(Vector2Normalize({ 1, 1 }), 350.0f); float ballR = 8.0f; const int cols = 10, rows = 5; Brick bricks[rows * cols]; for (int r = 0; r < rows; r++) for (int c = 0; c < cols; c++) bricks[r*cols + c] = { { 60.0f + c*70, 60.0f + r*30, 64, 22 }, true }; while (!WindowShouldClose()) { float dt = GetFrameTime(); if (IsKeyDown(KEY_LEFT)) paddle.x -= paddleSpeed * dt; if (IsKeyDown(KEY_RIGHT)) paddle.x += paddleSpeed * dt; paddle.x = Clamp(paddle.x, 0, W - paddle.width); ball = Vector2Add(ball, Vector2Scale(ballVel, dt)); if (ball.x < ballR || ball.x > W - ballR) ballVel.x *= -1; if (ball.y < ballR) ballVel.y *= -1; BeginDrawing(); ClearBackground({ 24,24,37,255 }); DrawRectangleRec(paddle, SKYBLUE); DrawCircleV(ball, ballR, RAYWHITE); for (auto& b : bricks) if (b.alive) DrawRectangleRec(b.rect, ORANGE); EndDrawing(); } CloseWindow(); return 0; }

Set the target FPS to 144 and then 30 — the ball crosses the screen in the same wall-clock time both ways. That is delta time working.

Normalize, then scale.
To launch a ball at a chosen speed in a chosen direction: Vector2Scale(Vector2Normalize(dir), speed). Never hand-pick component velocities like {350,350} — that is actually ~495px/s diagonally.
Beware a giant dt spike.
If the window is dragged or the game stalls, one frame's dt can be huge and a fast object can teleport through a wall ("tunneling"). Clamp it: dt = fminf(dt, 0.05f);. We solve tunneling properly with collision tests next lesson.
🎮 Build This: Breakout (movement phase)

A paddle and a velocity-vector ball moving with delta time toward a brick grid.

  1. Build the program above; control the paddle with arrow keys and confirm it clamps to the screen.
  2. Switch SetTargetFPS between 30, 60, and 144 — verify ball speed is identical.
  3. Add mouse control: set paddle.x = GetMousePosition().x - paddle.width/2; then clamp.
  4. Launch the ball in a random upward direction at start using Vector2Normalize.

title: Collision: AABB & Circle Tests
html:
The two collision shapes you need

Almost every 2D game gets by with two cheap tests: AABB (axis-aligned bounding box — rectangle vs rectangle) and circle (point/circle distance). raylib ships both so you rarely write the math, but you must understand it to respond correctly.

AABB: rectangle vs rectangle

Two rectangles overlap only if they overlap on both axes. If there is any gap on the X axis or any gap on the Y axis, they miss. raylib's function:

AABB test
bool hit = CheckCollisionRecs(rectA, rectB); // what it does under the hood: bool overlap = (a.x < b.x + b.width) && (a.x + a.width > b.x) && (a.y < b.y + b.height) && (a.y + a.height > b.y);

Circle tests

circle tests
bool cc = CheckCollisionCircles(c1, r1, c2, r2); // distance(c1,c2) < r1+r2 bool cr = CheckCollisionCircleRec(center, radius, rect); bool cp = CheckCollisionPointRec(point, rect);

Detection is easy — response is the craft

Knowing two things touch is half the job. The other half is reacting believably. For a ball hitting a brick, the key question is: which side did it hit? A common, robust approach is to compare how far the ball has penetrated on each axis and reflect the smaller one. A simpler heuristic that feels great in Breakout: decide the bounce axis from the ball's position relative to the brick center.

breakout.cpp (collision)
// inside the update loop, after moving the ball: for (auto& b : bricks) { if (!b.alive) continue; if (CheckCollisionCircleRec(ball, ballR, b.rect)) { b.alive = false; score += 10; // pick bounce axis: which overlap is smaller? float overlapL = (ball.x + ballR) - b.rect.x; float overlapR = (b.rect.x + b.rect.width) - (ball.x - ballR); float overlapT = (ball.y + ballR) - b.rect.y; float overlapB = (b.rect.y + b.rect.height) - (ball.y - ballR); float minX = fminf(overlapL, overlapR); float minY = fminf(overlapT, overlapB); if (minX < minY) ballVel.x *= -1; // hit a vertical face else ballVel.y *= -1; // hit a horizontal face break; // one brick per frame keeps bounces clean } } // paddle: reflect up, and steer by where it struck the paddle if (CheckCollisionCircleRec(ball, ballR, paddle) && ballVel.y > 0) { ballVel.y *= -1; float hit = (ball.x - (paddle.x + paddle.width/2)) / (paddle.width/2); ballVel.x = hit * 300.0f; // edge hits angle the ball outward ball.y = paddle.y - ballR; // nudge out so it can't re-trigger } if (ball.y > H) { lives--; /* reset ball */ } // missed the paddle

That paddle code is the trick that makes Breakout feel like a game and not a physics demo: the ball's horizontal velocity depends on where on the paddle it lands, so the player can aim.

Check the velocity direction before reflecting.
The guard ballVel.y > 0 means "only bounce if the ball is moving downward into the paddle". Without it, a ball already moving up that still overlaps the paddle flips again and gets stuck. The same idea applies to walls.
Always push the object out after a hit.
Detecting a collision and only flipping velocity can leave the ball still overlapping next frame — it re-collides and jitters or sticks. After reflecting, reposition it to just outside the surface (ball.y = paddle.y - ballR;). This is the single most common collision bug.
Detect, then resolve, then respond.
(1) Detect overlap. (2) Resolve by moving objects apart so they no longer overlap. (3) Respond by changing velocities / game state. Doing them in this order eliminates the classic "stuck inside a wall" class of bugs.

A note on broad performance

Checking every ball against every brick each frame (a few hundred tests) is trivially fast. When you scale to thousands of objects you add a broad phase (a grid or quadtree) to skip pairs that cannot possibly touch — but do not optimize that until a profiler tells you to.

🎮 Build This: Finish Breakout

Ball-vs-brick and ball-vs-paddle collisions that destroy bricks and reflect the ball.

  1. Add int score = 0; int lives = 3; and the collision block above to last lesson's Breakout.
  2. Destroy bricks on hit, increment score, and reflect on the correct axis.
  3. Make the paddle steer the ball based on hit position; reset the ball and lose a life when it drops below the screen.
  4. Draw score and lives with DrawText(TextFormat("Score %d Lives %d", score, lives), 10, 10, 20, WHITE);
  5. Show a "You Win" message when all bricks are dead and "Game Over" at zero lives.

title: Textures, Sprites & Animation
html:
From shapes to textures

Rectangles got us far, but real games draw images. In raylib an image on disk becomes a Texture2D (pixels living on the GPU). The lifecycle is always the same: load once before the loop, draw many times inside it, unload once at the end.

texture lifecycle
Texture2D snake = LoadTexture("assets/snake.png"); // after InitWindow! // inside the loop: DrawTexture(snake, x, y, WHITE); // at a position, full size DrawTextureV(snake, position, WHITE); // Vector2 position UnloadTexture(snake); // before CloseWindow

The WHITE argument is a tint: WHITE means "draw as-is". Tint with another color to fade or recolor; use {255,255,255,128} for 50% transparency.

Sprite sheets and source rectangles

Animation in 2D is a flipbook: several frames packed side by side in one image (a sprite sheet). You draw only one cell at a time using a source rectangle — the slice of the texture to copy — and a destination rectangle — where and how big to draw it on screen. DrawTexturePro handles both plus rotation:

frame stepping
Texture2D sheet = LoadTexture("assets/walk.png"); // 4 frames, 32px each = 128x32 int frameW = sheet.width / 4; // 32 int frameH = sheet.height; // 32 int frame = 0; float timer = 0, frameTime = 0.12f; // advance every 120ms // in the loop: timer += GetFrameTime(); if (timer >= frameTime) { timer = 0; frame = (frame + 1) % 4; } Rectangle src = { (float)frame * frameW, 0, (float)frameW, (float)frameH }; Rectangle dst = { posX, posY, frameW * 2.0f, frameH * 2.0f }; // 2x scale DrawTexturePro(sheet, src, dst, { 0, 0 }, 0.0f, WHITE);

The animation advances on a timer, not on every frame — again decoupled from frame rate via GetFrameTime(). To flip a sprite to face left, make the source rectangle's width negative: src.width = -frameW;.

Building Snake on a grid

Snake is the cleanest grid game. The world is a grid of cells (say 25x25 of 24px each). The snake is a list of grid cells; it moves one cell per tick (a fixed interval, not every frame), grows when it eats food, and dies if it hits a wall or itself.

snake.cpp (core)
#include "raylib.h" #include <deque> #include <raymath.h> const int CELL = 24, COLS = 25, ROWS = 25; int main() { InitWindow(COLS*CELL, ROWS*CELL, "Snake"); SetTargetFPS(60); Texture2D body = LoadTexture("assets/segment.png"); std::deque<Vector2> snake = { {12,12}, {11,12}, {10,12} }; Vector2 dir = { 1, 0 }; Vector2 food = { 5, 5 }; float tick = 0, tickRate = 0.15f; // snake steps 6.6x/sec while (!WindowShouldClose()) { // turn (block 180-degree reversals) if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_UP) && dir.y == 0) dir = { 0,-1 }; if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_DOWN) && dir.y == 0) dir = { 0, 1 }; if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_LEFT) && dir.x == 0) dir = { -1,0 }; if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_RIGHT) && dir.x == 0) dir = { 1, 0 }; tick += GetFrameTime(); if (tick >= tickRate) { tick = 0; Vector2 head = Vector2Add(snake.front(), dir); snake.push_front(head); if (head.x == food.x && head.y == food.y) { food = { (float)GetRandomValue(0,COLS-1), (float)GetRandomValue(0,ROWS-1) }; } else { snake.pop_back(); // no growth: drop the tail } } BeginDrawing(); ClearBackground({ 20,30,20,255 }); DrawRectangle(food.x*CELL, food.y*CELL, CELL, CELL, RED); for (auto& s : snake) DrawTexture(body, s.x*CELL, s.y*CELL, WHITE); EndDrawing(); } UnloadTexture(body); CloseWindow(); return 0; }

The growth logic is the elegant part: every tick we push a new head; if we ate, we keep the tail so the body lengthens; otherwise we drop the tail so the snake appears to slide.

No art? Use placeholders.
Ship gameplay first with DrawRectangle, then swap in textures. raylib can also GenImageColor / LoadTextureFromImage at runtime so you never block on an artist.
Move on ticks, animate on time, never on frames.
If the snake stepped every frame it would be unplayably fast at 144 FPS. Accumulate GetFrameTime() and act when the timer crosses your interval. The same accumulator pattern drives sprite-frame stepping.
🎮 Build This: Snake

A grid snake drawn from segment textures that grows when it eats food.

  1. Build the core above; confirm the snake slides, turns, and grows on food.
  2. Add death: end the game if the head leaves the grid or matches any body cell.
  3. Use a sprite sheet for an animated head (frame-step its source rectangle) and rotate it to face dir.
  4. Add a score = snake length minus 3, drawn with DrawText, and a restart on KEY_ENTER.

title: Sound & Structuring a Game with Entities & State
html:
Audio in raylib

Sound needs an audio device, initialized once after the window. Short effects load as Sound (decompressed in memory, instant playback); long music streams as Music (decoded as it plays, must be ticked each frame).

audio
InitAudioDevice(); // after InitWindow Sound shoot = LoadSound("assets/shoot.wav"); Sound thrust = LoadSound("assets/thrust.wav"); Music bgm = LoadMusicStream("assets/song.ogg"); PlayMusicStream(bgm); // in the loop: UpdateMusicStream(bgm); // REQUIRED every frame for music if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_SPACE)) PlaySound(shoot); SetSoundVolume(thrust, 0.4f); // at shutdown: UnloadSound(shoot); UnloadMusicStream(bgm); CloseAudioDevice();

Entities: stop using loose variables

So far each object was a pile of separate variables. That does not scale to dozens of asteroids and bullets. Group related data into a struct — the entity — and keep many of them in a std::vector.

entities
struct Ship { Vector2 pos, vel; float rot; }; struct Bullet { Vector2 pos, vel; float life; bool alive; }; struct Asteroid{ Vector2 pos, vel; float radius; bool alive; }; Ship ship = { { 400, 300 }, { 0, 0 }, 0 }; std::vector<Bullet> bullets; std::vector<Asteroid> asteroids;

Updating becomes a loop over the vector, and "despawning" is just setting alive = false then removing dead entities once per frame.

A simple game-state machine

A real game is not always "playing" — it has a menu, the gameplay, and a game-over screen. Model that explicitly with an enum and a switch. This is the single most valuable structural pattern for small games.

state machine
enum class GameState { Menu, Playing, GameOver }; GameState state = GameState::Menu; while (!WindowShouldClose()) { float dt = GetFrameTime(); UpdateMusicStream(bgm); BeginDrawing(); ClearBackground(BLACK); switch (state) { case GameState::Menu: DrawText("ASTEROIDS", 280, 200, 50, WHITE); DrawText("Press ENTER to play", 290, 270, 20, GRAY); if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_ENTER)) { ResetGame(); state = GameState::Playing; } break; case GameState::Playing: // thrust if (IsKeyDown(KEY_UP)) { Vector2 fwd = { sinf(ship.rot*DEG2RAD), -cosf(ship.rot*DEG2RAD) }; ship.vel = Vector2Add(ship.vel, Vector2Scale(fwd, 250*dt)); if (!IsSoundPlaying(thrust)) PlaySound(thrust); } if (IsKeyDown(KEY_LEFT)) ship.rot -= 200*dt; if (IsKeyDown(KEY_RIGHT)) ship.rot += 200*dt; if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_SPACE)) { Vector2 fwd = { sinf(ship.rot*DEG2RAD), -cosf(ship.rot*DEG2RAD) }; bullets.push_back({ ship.pos, Vector2Scale(fwd, 500), 1.5f, true }); PlaySound(shoot); } UpdateWorld(dt); if (asteroids.empty() || playerDead) state = GameState::GameOver; DrawWorld(); break; case GameState::GameOver: DrawText("GAME OVER", 270, 220, 50, RED); if (IsKeyPressed(KEY_ENTER)) state = GameState::Menu; break; } EndDrawing(); }

Notice each state owns its input, update, and draw. Switching states is one assignment. Adding a Pause state later is just a new enum value and case.

Screen wrap is the Asteroids feel.
Make objects that leave one edge appear on the opposite: if (pos.x < 0) pos.x += W; if (pos.x > W) pos.x -= W; (same for Y). Apply it to the ship, bullets, and asteroids.
Removing entities mid-loop invalidates iterators.
Do not erase from a vector while a range-for is iterating it. Mark alive = false, then sweep once at the end of the frame with the erase-remove idiom: v.erase(std::remove_if(v.begin(), v.end(), [](auto& e){ return !e.alive; }), v.end());
Update then draw, never interleave.
Finish all state changes for the frame before any draw call. Drawing mid-update gives objects that lag a frame behind each other and produces visual jitter that is maddening to debug.
🎮 Build This: Asteroids Prototype

A ship and asteroid entities with thrust/shoot sounds and Menu/Play/GameOver states.

  1. Define the three entity structs and the GameState enum; wire up the state switch above.
  2. Implement ship rotation + thrust (with screen wrap) and firing bullets that expire via life -= dt.
  3. Spawn a handful of drifting asteroids; play shoot on fire and thrust while accelerating.
  4. Transition Menu → Playing on ENTER and Playing → GameOver when the player dies. Collisions and scoring come next lesson.

title: Capstone: Asteroids & Distributing the Build
html:
Completing the game

The prototype has movement, sound, and states. A game needs goals and stakes: scoring, lives, and the loop of breaking big asteroids into smaller ones. Use the circle-collision skill from Lesson 5 — every Asteroids object is a circle.

collisions + scoring
int score = 0, lives = 3; void SplitAsteroid(std::vector<Asteroid>& a, const Asteroid& hit) { if (hit.radius < 20) return; // smallest size: just vanishes float r = hit.radius / 2; for (int i = 0; i < 2; i++) a.push_back({ hit.pos, { (float)GetRandomValue(-120,120), (float)GetRandomValue(-120,120) }, r, true }); } // bullet vs asteroid for (auto& b : bullets) for (auto& a : asteroids) { if (b.alive && a.alive && CheckCollisionCircles(b.pos, 2, a.pos, a.radius)) { b.alive = false; a.alive = false; score += (a.radius > 40) ? 20 : (a.radius > 20 ? 50 : 100); SplitAsteroid(asteroids, a); PlaySound(boom); } } // ship vs asteroid for (auto& a : asteroids) if (a.alive && CheckCollisionCircles(ship.pos, 12, a.pos, a.radius)) { lives--; ship.pos = { W/2.0f, H/2.0f }; ship.vel = {0,0}; if (lives <= 0) state = GameState::GameOver; }

Smaller asteroids score more (they are harder to hit), and clearing them all can spawn a fresh, faster wave — that is your difficulty curve. Draw score and lives with DrawText(TextFormat(...)) in the HUD.

Building a Release binary

Debug builds are big and slow. For anything you hand to a player, build in Release mode — the optimizer turns on and the binary shrinks:

release build
$ cmake -S . -B build -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release $ cmake --build build --config Release [100%] Built target game

Statically linking raylib (the default with FetchContent) means the library is baked into your executable — no separate .dll/.so to ship. That is exactly what you want for distribution.

Packaging: assets travel with the binary

The most common "works on my machine" failure: the game can't find assets/. raylib resolves relative paths against the working directory, not the executable. Two fixes — use both:

  • Ship the assets/ folder next to the executable in the zip.
  • At startup, set the working directory to the executable's folder so double-clicking works: ChangeDirectory(GetApplicationDirectory());
distribution layout
Asteroids-v1.0-mac/ ├── game # or game.exe on Windows └── assets/ ├── shoot.wav ├── thrust.wav └── boom.wav $ zip -r Asteroids-v1.0-mac.zip Asteroids-v1.0-mac/

Per-platform notes

PlatformYou shipGotcha
Windowsgame.exe + assetsStatic-link the C++ runtime, or users hit "missing VCRUNTIME140.dll".
macOSApp bundle or zipUnsigned apps trigger Gatekeeper; users right-click → Open the first time.
LinuxBinary + assets + run scriptBuild on an older glibc for forward compatibility.
Webraylib + Emscripten → HTML/WASMBest reach; runs in any browser, no install.
Web is the easiest "send to a friend".
raylib compiles to WebAssembly with Emscripten (emcmake cmake .. && emmake make), producing an .html + .wasm + .data you can host anywhere — no installer, instant play.
Test the zip on a clean machine.
Always unzip and run on a computer that never had your dev tools. Missing runtimes, missing assets, and absolute paths you forgot all surface here — not on the machine where everything is already installed.
Version and changelog every build.
Name artifacts with a version (Asteroids-v1.0). When a player reports a bug, you must know exactly which build they ran.
🎮 Build This: Asteroids Capstone + Ship It

A complete asteroids-style shooter, then a distributable build.

  1. Add bullet-vs-asteroid and ship-vs-asteroid collisions; split large asteroids into two smaller ones on hit.
  2. Track score and lives, draw a HUD, and end the game at zero lives; spawn a faster new wave when all asteroids are cleared.
  3. Call ChangeDirectory(GetApplicationDirectory()) at startup so assets load when double-clicked.
  4. Build with -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release, place the binary beside assets/, and zip it with a version name.
  5. Unzip and run it on a different machine to confirm it is truly self-contained, then share it.
